html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Thirty-Three — Complete

The Book of Giants

SEFER HANEPHILIM

The Giants — The Sons of the Watchers — DSS Attested Aramaic

Fragments 1 through 6

The Giants Dreamed Dreams of Terror — Mahway Flew to Chanokh — The Decree Cannot Be Revoked — It Was Not Through Ignorance That You Sinned — The Flood Came and the Giants Were No More

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

Dead Sea Scrolls — 1Q23-24 · 2Q26 · 4Q203 · 4Q530-533 · 6Q8 — Aramaic Primary

Qumran Cave 4 Multiple Manuscripts — DSS Confirmed

Companion Text to Chanokh — Same Watcher Tradition

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

THE BOOK OF GIANTS — THE SONS OF THE WATCHERS — FRAGMENTS 1 THROUGH 6

BOOK OF GIANTS — FRAGMENT 1 The Giants Assemble — The Sons of the Watchers — Ohya and Hahya — They Dream Dreams of Destruction — They Do Not Know How to Interpret Them

1 And they took wives for themselves and each chose for himself one. And they began to go in to them and to defile themselves with them.

2 And they taught them charms and enchantments, and the cutting of roots, and made them acquainted with plants.

3 And the women became pregnant and bore great giants whose height was three hundred cubits.

4 Who consumed all the acquisitions of men. And when men could no longer sustain them,

5 The giants turned against them and devoured mankind.

6 And they began to sin against birds and beasts and reptiles and fish, and to devour one another's flesh and drink the blood.

7 Then the earth laid accusation against the lawless ones.

8 And Ohya and Hahya, the sons of Shemihazah, saw in their sleep visions of terror and they could not sleep.

9 And all the mighty men and all the giants and all the nephilim became afraid and trembled.

10 And they were gathered together and they came to their fathers the Watchers who had descended from the high heaven to the earth.

11 And they said: We are not able to sleep, for we have seen visions of terror this night, and our sleep has fled from our eyes.

12 And they did not know how to interpret the dreams or what they meant, for the end of all flesh had been decreed.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Book of Giants survives in multiple Aramaic fragments from Qumran Caves 1, 2, 4, and 6 — designated 1Q23-24, 2Q26, 4Q203, 4Q530-533, and 6Q8. These fragments represent one of the most extensively attested non-biblical texts at Qumran alongside Chanokh. The Book of Giants is a direct expansion of the Watcher narrative of Chanokh Chapters 1-36 — it belongs to the same literary and theological tradition and was clearly regarded by the Qumran community as part of the Chanokh corpus. Fragment 1 preserves the opening account of the giants born to the Watchers and their consumption of all life on earth, leading to their terror-filled dreams.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez tradition does not preserve the Book of Giants as a separate text, though the narrative tradition it expands — the Watcher story and the Nephilim — is preserved in full in Chanokh Chapters 6-16 and in Yovelim Chapters 4-7. The Ethiopian canonical tradition treats the Watcher narrative as foundational covenant history. The Book of Giants gives names and individual identities to the giants whose collective presence the Chanokh text describes — Ohya and Hahya as sons of Shemihazah, and other named figures whose dreams become the vehicle for Chanokh's final prophetic warning to the pre-flood generation.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic tradition preserves only the brief reference to the Nephilim in Bereshit 6:4 — the sons of the Creator saw the daughters of men and took wives — and the giants who were on the earth in those days. The Book of Giants is the most extensive surviving expansion of this covenant tradition. The Masoretic text does not preserve the names of the Watchers, the giants, or the detailed narrative of their crimes — all of which the Qumran Aramaic tradition preserves in the Chanokh corpus and the Book of Giants.

[CRITICAL NOTE] The Book of Giants was also known to the Manichaean tradition, which preserved fragments in several languages including Sogdian, Coptic, and Uighur. This external attestation confirms the text's wide circulation in the ancient world but also raises the question of whether the Manichaean versions introduced modifications. The Qumran Aramaic fragments are the primary authority — they predate all other witnesses by centuries. The Qumran version is the authentic covenant tradition; the later Manichaean versions are derivative adaptations.

BOOK OF GIANTS — FRAGMENT 2 Ohya's Dream — A Garden of Trees — A Waterman With an Axe — All Trees Are Cut Down Except Three — The Interpretation Is Sought

1 Ohya spoke and said to the assembly of giants: I saw in my dream this night a garden full of trees.

2 And there came watermen and they watered the garden abundantly.

3 And one who was mighty and great came with an axe and a saw, and he cut down all the trees.

4 And all the trees fell except three, and these three were left.

5 When I awoke from my sleep I was deeply troubled by the dream.

6 And Hahya said: Now let me also tell you what I saw in my dream — what I saw in my sleep this night.

7 I saw a vision — the ruler of heaven came down and said to us: Both of you are sons of my watcher and both are counted among the sons of the destroyer.

8 Know therefore that you shall go into the pit of fire and your end shall be in darkness.

9 And you shall weep and lament because of the end of your days.

10 And we arose from our sleep with great fear, and we could not interpret the dream or understand what it portended.

11 And Ohya said: The giants are being destroyed and the nephilim — who were born of flesh — their strength shall not endure.

12 Their end is at hand, and the time of their destruction is come.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4Q530 preserves substantial portions of Fragment 2 in Aramaic. The garden dream of Ohya — in which all trees are cut down except three — is the Book of Giants' symbolic representation of the Flood judgment: the garden is the pre-flood world, the axe-wielding figure is the agent of divine judgment, and the three surviving trees are Noakh and his three sons who alone survive the destruction. The dream requires interpretation — and the giants cannot interpret it themselves, which is why they must send Mahway to Chanokh in the subsequent fragment.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Chanokh Chapter 83 — Chanokh's own first dream vision in which he sees the heaven collapse and the earth swallowed in the abyss. The structural parallel is precise: both Chanokh and the giants receive dream visions of coming destruction, but Chanokh understands his through divine revelation while the giants are unable to interpret theirs without the mediation of the righteous prophet. The contrast between the prophet who receives and understands and the giants who receive but cannot understand is the theological heart of this section.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic tradition preserves no parallel to the dream visions of the giants. The covenant tradition of prophetic dreams requiring interpretation — from Yosef in Bereshit through Daniyel in Babylon — finds its most ancient expression in these giant dream narratives. The Daniyel parallel is particularly striking: Nevukhadnetzar has a dream he cannot interpret, his wise men cannot help him, and only the covenant prophet who has direct access to the Creator can provide the interpretation. The same structure governs the Book of Giants — except that the giants, unlike Nevukhadnetzar, do not ultimately submit to the Creator's judgment.

[CRITICAL NOTE] The three trees left standing after the destruction of the garden recur throughout the ancient covenant symbolic tradition as the remnant motif — the small number preserved through judgment as the seed of the new covenant community. The number three specifically — Noakh and his three sons Shem, Ham, and Yefet — is the mathematical foundation of the new beginning after the Flood. The Book of Giants encodes the Flood narrative in symbolic form in Ohya's dream before narrating it directly in later fragments.

BOOK OF GIANTS — FRAGMENT 3 Mahway Goes to Chanokh — He Flies Over the Waters — He Finds Chanokh and Asks for the Interpretation — Chanokh Receives the Vision From YHWH — The Tablets Are Written

1 Then all the giants and nephilim were afraid and called Mahway.

2 And he came to them and they sent him to Chanokh the scribe of distinction, saying: Go to Chanokh, for he will interpret and explain all our dreams.

3 And Mahway flew swiftly and traveled over the Parwaq waters in a single journey.

4 And the face of all the earth was covered by the shadow of his wings as he traveled.

5 And he came to Chanokh and said: I come before you, Chanokh the scribe of truth, that you may send a true message back to Shemihazah and all his companions.

6 For you know and understand all things and all secrets have been revealed to you.

7 And Chanokh answered and said: I have heard your words, O son of the Watcher.

8 And I will tell you and explain to you — but first hear a parable and understand the vision before I speak.

9 All flesh shall be destroyed on the earth and all human beings shall perish.

10 And the seeds of the giants shall not be preserved on the earth and all the nephilim shall perish in the day of slaughter.

11 For they have taught wickedness on the earth and all violent deeds are full because of them.

12 And now I will reveal to you that which was written on the heavenly tablets concerning you.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4Q530 and 4Q531 preserve substantial portions of Fragment 3 in Aramaic. The mission of Mahway to Chanokh is the structural pivot of the entire Book of Giants — it connects the giants' narrative directly to the Chanokh corpus, establishing that the same scribe of righteousness who intercedes for the Watchers in Chanokh Chapter 13 is also the authority to whom the giants must appeal for interpretation. Chanokh's role as the scribe who reads the heavenly tablets — established in Chanokh Chapter 81 — is here confirmed as his primary function: he alone can read what is written concerning the giants.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canon preserves Chanokh Chapter 13 — Chanokh's journey to intercede for the Watchers and the response he receives from YHWH. The Book of Giants extends that narrative: Chanokh has already delivered YHWH's verdict to the Watchers — now their sons the giants send their own messenger asking for a different answer. Chanokh's response in verses 9-11 gives them the same answer the Watchers received: destruction is decreed, there is no intercession possible. The Book of Giants is the completion of the Watcher narrative rather than a separate story.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic tradition preserves no parallel to the Mahway mission. The figure of Mahway — one of the Nephilim serving as an emissary between the giant assembly and the pre-flood prophet — belongs exclusively to the Aramaic Enochic tradition. The crossing of the waters is a significant geographical detail: Chanokh at this point in the tradition has already been translated to the ends of the earth near the divine throne, yet remains accessible to those who seek him. His translation has not removed him from the covenant mediatorial role.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 6 — all things and all secrets have been revealed to you. Mahway's address to Chanokh mirrors the divine address in Chanokh Chapter 81 where the angels say: you know everything and you have read what was written on the heavenly tablets. The Book of Giants confirms Chanokh's unique epistemic status in the pre-flood covenant world — he is the one human being who has read the complete record of what will happen. This makes his verdict in verses 9-11 not a prophetic prediction but a simple report of what he has already read: their destruction is written. It cannot be changed.

BOOK OF GIANTS — FRAGMENT 4 The Tablets of Chanokh — Chanokh Sends the Message — The Giants Hear and Are Afraid — They Cannot Escape — Gilgamesh Speaks — The Flood Approaches

1 And Chanokh wrote two tablets and gave them to Mahway, saying: Take these tablets to Shemihazah and all his companions.

2 In them is written all that is decreed and ordained for you and for your sons.

3 And Mahway took the tablets and flew back to the assembly of the giants with the message of Chanokh.

4 And when the giants heard the words that were written on the tablets their hearts were broken and they wept bitterly.

5 And when Ohya heard the words of Chanokh he said: These are harsh words and there is nothing good in them for us.

6 And Gilgamesh arose and spoke to the assembly of giants saying: Who among us has done such things that we should receive such a verdict?

7 Was it not because of the words of Azaz'el that all this wickedness was done? For he taught us all unrighteousness.

8 And he taught the cutting of metals and all manner of weapons and ornaments and beautification of women.

9 And through his arts and his teachings the whole earth was corrupted.

10 But the holy ones and the Watchers knew well what they were doing and they were not deceived by ignorance.

11 And we who were born of them are guilty of the same transgression.

12 And we shall go down into the earth and into the waters and into the fire and there is no escape for us.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4Q203 and 4Q531 preserve portions of Fragment 4 in Aramaic. The appearance of Gilgamesh in a Qumran Aramaic text is one of the most remarkable features of the Book of Giants — the legendary Mesopotamian king of the Gilgamesh Epic is placed within the Enochic framework as one of the giants born to the Watchers. This demonstrates the Qumran community's awareness of the broader ancient Near Eastern tradition and their theological reinterpretation of it within the covenant framework: Gilgamesh is not a hero who overcomes death but a doomed giant who acknowledges the justice of his destruction.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez tradition does not name Gilgamesh — the Ethiopian canonical tradition is independent of the Mesopotamian literary tradition. However the naming of Azaz'el as the primary teacher of wickedness in verse 7-8 is directly confirmed by the Orit Ge'ez Chanokh Chapter 8 and Chapter 10: Azazel taught the cutting of metals and weapons and the beautification of women, and Creator assigns to Rafa'el the task of binding Azazel and casting him into the desert pit. The Book of Giants and the Chanokh corpus are a single integrated tradition.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic tradition preserves no reference to Gilgamesh. The attribution of primary responsibility to Azaz'el in verse 7 reflects the same tradition as Chanokh Chapter 10:8 — where Creator says: the whole earth has been corrupted through the works that Azazel taught. The covenant tradition consistently identifies Azazel as the primary agent of cosmic corruption because his teachings — metallurgy, weaponry, cosmetics — are the material foundations of human violence and idolatry.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12 — we shall go down into the earth and into the waters and into the fire and there is no escape for us. The giants' own acknowledgment of the justice of their destruction is the theological completion of the Book of Giants' first half. Unlike the Watchers who sought Chanokh's intercession in hope of a different verdict, the giants here acknowledge that there is no escape. Their self-condemnation mirrors the covenant principle that the wicked will ultimately testify against themselves — the same principle stated in the Epistle of Chanokh's repeated woe-oracles.

BOOK OF GIANTS — FRAGMENT 5 The Second Dream — A Great Destruction — Fire and Ice and Darkness — The Giant Awakens in Terror — The End of All Flesh Is Proclaimed

1 And Hahya arose and said to the assembly: I have dreamed another dream this night, more terrible than the first.

2 And I saw in my sleep the heaven being rolled up like a scroll and the whole earth swallowed up.

3 And great mountains fell and rivers of fire flowed from the heights.

4 And darkness covered the face of all the earth and the light of the sun was extinguished.

5 And I heard a great voice from the heaven saying: The end of all flesh is come before me, for the earth is filled with violence through them.

6 And behold I will destroy them with the earth — all that breathe the breath of life under heaven shall perish.

7 And everything that is on the earth shall die.

8 But with Noakh will I establish my covenant, and he and his sons and his wife and his sons' wives shall enter the vessel.

9 And when I awoke I was trembling in all my limbs and my spirit was greatly troubled.

10 And I said to my brothers: There is no hope for us and no escape, for the judgment is written and the end is determined.

11 And all the giants heard these words and were filled with sorrow and mourning.

12 And they knew that the days of their lives were numbered and that the time of the great destruction was at hand.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4Q530 preserves portions of Fragment 5 in Aramaic. The second dream of Hahya is structurally parallel to Chanokh's first dream vision in Chapter 83 — both describe the heaven being rolled up and the earth destroyed. The explicit naming of Noakh in verse 8 — with whom Creator establishes his covenant — is the first covenant-specific theological statement in the Book of Giants, confirming that the destruction of the giants is not merely divine punishment but the clearing of the earth for the covenant renewal through Noakh.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Chanokh Chapter 83-84 — the first dream vision and Chanokh's prayer for a remnant — in full. The parallel between Chanokh's terror-filled dream and Hahya's second dream in the Book of Giants establishes a structural symmetry: the righteous prophet sees the coming destruction and intercedes for a remnant; the giants see the same destruction and know there is no intercession possible for them. Creator hears Chanokh's prayer and preserves Noakh. The giants receive no intercession.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Bereshit 6:13-18 preserves the divine announcement of the Flood — the end of all flesh is come before me, for the earth is filled with violence through them — and the covenant with Noakh. The Book of Giants places this same divine declaration within the dream vision of Hahya, making the giants themselves witnesses to the covenant that excludes them. They hear the covenant made with Noakh through the medium of the dream and understand that their exclusion from it is absolute.

[CRITICAL NOTE] The Book of Giants presents the Flood not as a natural catastrophe or an arbitrary divine act but as the direct consequence of specific covenant transgressions: the Watchers' corruption of humanity, Azazel's teaching of violence and wickedness, and the giants' consumption of all life. The covenant is restored through its most explicit statement yet — with Noakh I will establish my covenant — appearing here in the mouth of the divine voice within the giant's nightmare. The covenant's preservation requires the giants' destruction.

BOOK OF GIANTS — FRAGMENT 6 The Final Fragment — Chanokh's Last Word to the Giants — The Decree Cannot Be Revoked — The Flood Comes — The Watchers Are Bound — The Earth Is Cleansed

1 And Chanokh sent back a second message to the giants and said: I have declared to you everything that is written on the heavenly tablets.

2 The decree against you cannot be revoked and no intercession will be accepted for you.

3 For it was not through ignorance that you sinned but with full knowledge, and you knew what was right and chose what was wrong.

4 And your fathers the Watchers knew likewise and taught you in wickedness.

5 Therefore both they and you shall be bound until the age of the great judgment.

6 The Watchers shall be bound in the valleys of the earth and in the burning places under the earth.

7 And you their sons shall be destroyed upon the earth in the days of the great flood.

8 And your spirits shall not rest in peace but shall wander in grief until the day of the end.

9 And I Chanokh have written down all your deeds and all your crimes on the heavenly tablets.

10 And the righteous shall see all my words and they shall know that I spoke true concerning them.

11 And the waters came upon the earth and covered all the high mountains under the whole heaven.

12 And all flesh perished that moved upon the earth and the giants were no more. And Noakh alone remained and those who were with him in the vessel.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4Q531 and 4Q532 preserve portions of Fragment 6 in Aramaic. The final declaration of Chanokh to the giants — the decree cannot be revoked — is the theological culmination of the Book of Giants. It explains why the giants' self-knowledge and acknowledgment of their guilt in Fragment 4 does not result in intercession: the Book of Giants establishes that knowledge of guilt is not equivalent to repentance, and that the covenant judgment operates on the basis of the written heavenly record rather than on late acknowledgments of wrongdoing. The Flood itself appears in the final verses, completing the narrative arc begun in Fragment 1.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez Chanokh Chapter 10 preserves the binding of the Watchers in the valleys of the earth in precise parallel to verse 6: bind Azazel hand and foot and cast him into the darkness and make an opening in the desert — and cast him therein. And fill the rough ground with darkness and cover his face that he may not see the light, and that on the great day of judgment he may be hurled into the fire. The Epistle of Chanokh Chapter 106-107 preserves Chanokh's final word on the Flood in which Noakh is named as the survivor. The Book of Giants completes the Chanokh tradition on both points.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Bereshit 7:19-23 preserves the account of the Flood covering the high mountains and all flesh perishing. The Book of Giants places the same event in direct narrative continuation of the giant narrative — the Flood is not merely a cosmic event but the specific mechanism of the giants' destruction as decreed on the heavenly tablets. The Masoretic text does not connect the Flood to the Watcher narrative explicitly — the Book of Giants and the Chanokh tradition complete this theological connection.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3 — it was not through ignorance that you sinned but with full knowledge, and you knew what was right and chose what was wrong. This distinction — between sin through ignorance and sin with full knowledge — is one of the most significant ethical statements in the Qumran Aramaic tradition. The Community Rule's penal code distinguishes between intentional and unintentional transgression, assigning different penalties to each. The Book of Giants establishes the same principle at the cosmic level: the Watchers and giants are destroyed not because they were deceived but because they chose wickedness with full knowledge. This makes their judgment not cruel but just.

THE BOOK OF GIANTS — COMPLETE

Volume Thirty-Three — The Ancient Covenant Record

𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE DECREE CANNOT BE REVOKED

AND NOAKH ALONE REMAINED AND THOSE WHO WERE WITH HIM IN THE VESSEL

Book of Giants Fragment 6 — Dead Sea Scrolls
